questions.question: Lamport clocks assign each event a single integer. If LC(a) = 3 and LC(b) = 5, what can you conclude?
options: Event a definitely happened before event b
Event b definitely happened before event a
Event a may or may not have happened before event b — the relationship is inconclusive
Events a and b are concurrent
questions.explanation: LC(a) < LC(b) only tells you that a → b is *possible*, not that it *did* happen. Two independent events can have LC values 3 and 5. The single integer collapses all causal information — you lose the structure that distinguishes 'a caused b' from 'they happened independently'.

questions.question: When a process receives a message, how should it update its vector clock?
options: Set its own counter to the received vector's value plus one
Take the element-wise maximum of local and received vectors, then increment the sender's counter
Take the element-wise maximum of local and received vectors, then increment its own counter
Increment only its own counter by one; ignore the received vector
questions.explanation: On receive: merge (element-wise max) to incorporate all causal knowledge from the sender, then increment *your own* counter to record this receive event. This is the key difference from Lamport clocks — you preserve per-process information instead of collapsing to a single counter.

questions.question: Two versions of a key have vector clocks VC₁ = [2, 0, 1] and VC₂ = [0, 3, 1]. What is their relationship and what should a conflict detector conclude?
options: VC₁ < VC₂: version 1 causally precedes version 2; no conflict
VC₁ || VC₂ (concurrent): neither caused the other; this is a conflict requiring resolution
VC₁ > VC₂: version 1 causally supersedes version 2; no conflict
They are identical: both describe the same causal state
questions.explanation: VC₁[0]=2 > VC₂[0]=0 but VC₁[1]=0 < VC₂[1]=3. Neither vector dominates the other element-wise, so they are incomparable (concurrent). This means the two updates happened independently — neither caused the other — and a conflict detector must flag this for resolution.

questions.question: In causal broadcast (CBCAST), when does a process deliver a received message to the application?
options: Immediately upon receiving it, then update its vector clock
After incrementing its own vector clock counter once for the receive
Only after all causally preceding messages (tracked via vector clock comparison) have been delivered
After confirming with the sender that the message was not a duplicate
questions.explanation: CBCAST buffers messages whose causal predecessors haven't arrived yet. A message m from process Pᵢ with VC[m] = [v₁, v₂, …, vₙ] can be delivered only when the recipient has seen at least vⱼ messages from every Pⱼ where j ≠ i, and exactly vᵢ − 1 messages from Pᵢ. This ensures that all of m's causal predecessors are delivered first.

questions.question: Why do dotted version vectors (DVVs) add a single (replica_id, counter) pair on top of a regular vector clock?
options: To reduce the size of the vector clock in messages
To distinguish which specific event created a version when the same replica makes multiple updates between merges — plain VCs lose this information
To enforce a total ordering on all events across all replicas
To detect Byzantine failures by cross-checking the dot against the vector
questions.explanation: Consider replica R that writes twice between merges. Both writes extend R's counter in the VC. If another replica merges these versions, the VC only shows 'R has count ≥ 2' — it can't tell which of the two events created a particular version. The dot (R, 2) in a DVV says 'this version was specifically R's 2nd event,' disambiguating cases where plain VCs would conflate distinct events.

questions.question: Which conflict resolution strategy provides mathematically guaranteed convergence without data loss, but restricts you to specific data types?
options: Last-writer-wins (LWW) with a global clock
Application-specific merge functions
Conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs)
Manual resolution by exposing both versions to the user
questions.explanation: CRDTs are data structures (counters, sets, registers) whose merge operation is commutative, associative, and idempotent. This guarantees convergence regardless of message ordering. The trade-off is that you can only perform operations the CRDT supports — you can't write arbitrary merge logic. LWW loses data; application merge is correct but ad-hoc; manual resolution is flexible but poor UX.
